title: Quick check: severity bands and SLAs

questions.prompt: A new tech says: "This incident looks Low to me, not High. I'll re-grade it." What's the right correction?
choices: Re-grading is the SOC's call. Asking the analyst to revisit is fine; doing it yourself unilaterally is second-guessing the recommendation.

choices.label: Re-grading is encouraged when the tech has more local context than the SOC.
choices.rationale: Customer context goes back to the analyst in a reply; it doesn't justify unilateral re-grading.

choices.label: Re-grading is fine as long as you document it.
choices.rationale: Documentation doesn't make unilateral override correct.

choices.label: Re-grading is impossible — the portal locks the field.
choices.rationale: The lesson doesn't say the field is locked; it says you don't re-grade regardless.

questions.explanation: Severity is the SOC's call. Ask, don't override.

questions.prompt: "Low severity means I can ignore it." Why is that misconception harmful?
choices: Low means the SOC confirmed it as worth your time, just not at the Critical tempo; ignoring Low is how queues become backlogs.

choices.label: Because the SLA on Low is tighter than on High.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the SLA shape. Low's SLA is typically the most permissive.

choices.label: Because Low incidents are usually graded incorrectly.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the misconception's cause.

choices.label: Because Low incidents are billed at a higher rate.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the commercial model.

questions.explanation: Low is normal tempo, not ignore.

questions.prompt: A High incident lands after hours. What does the lesson recommend as the typical pattern?

choices.label: Wake the senior; High is always wake-the-senior after-hours.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Critical wakes someone; High waits for the morning at most MSPs.

choices: Note the incident; action it in the morning unless your MSP's after-hours policy says otherwise.

choices.label: Action it immediately yourself, regardless of the time.
choices.rationale: High doesn't have an immediate-action requirement; it has a customer-SLA clock.

choices.label: Close it as benign; High after-hours is usually a false positive.
choices.rationale: Worst response. Closing without action and without dialogue is the cardinal mistake.

questions.explanation: Typical pattern: High waits for the morning. Check your MSP's policy for the exact rule.

questions.prompt: Critical incidents have a "different response model from Low and High." What's the difference?
choices: Contain first (isolate or run the compromise playbook), escalate in parallel, do NOT investigate.

choices.label: Investigate first to confirm severity, then contain.
choices.rationale: Investigation before containment on a Critical is exactly what the lesson prevents.

choices.label: Same response shape as High but with a 1-hour SLA.
choices.rationale: The response shape itself differs; it isn't a faster High.

choices.label: Wait for the senior to take over completely; don't take any action yourself.
choices.rationale: The tech contains in parallel with escalating.

questions.explanation: Forensics and persistence-hunting are above the helpdesk ceiling. Contain and escalate; don't investigate.

questions.prompt: 4:30pm Friday. You finish at 5pm; on-call starts at 6pm. Incident A: High EDR, encoded PowerShell scheduled task on a file server. Incident B: Low EDR, autoruns from an obscure software vendor. What do you do?

choices.label: Work both before 5pm — they're both your responsibility.
choices.rationale: Rushing both at the end of a shift is how mistakes happen on the more serious one.

choices: Work the High before 5pm. Leave the Low in the queue for Monday.

choices.label: Escalate both to the on-call rotation as the shift ends.
choices.rationale: On-call is for incidents that arrive after hours or outpace your shift.

choices.label: Reduce the High to a Low to fit them both into your time.
choices.rationale: Re-grading is the SOC's call, not a queue-management tool.

questions.explanation: High has the tighter SLA. Low can wait two-and-a-half business days without harm.

questions.prompt: Continuing the Friday High: while working it, the customer's IT contact says they think the scheduled task was created during a software install last night. They're confident it's benign. What do you do?

choices.label: Close as benign on the customer's word and move on.
choices.rationale: The customer's context is new signal, not a disposition. Same family as second-guessing the SOC.

choices: Reply to the analyst with the new context, leave the incident open, finish your shift, hand to on-call with a note.

choices.label: Reduce the severity from High to Low yourself, given the customer's confidence.
choices.rationale: Re-grading on a customer claim is two mistakes in one click.

choices.label: Approve the remediation immediately to clear the ticket before 5pm.
choices.rationale: Acting before the analyst weighs the new context is the angry-follow-up pattern.

questions.explanation: Signal goes to the layer that decides; the ticket doesn't get dropped on the floor.

questions.prompt: Huntress's severity bands map to your MSP's SLAs. Where do the actual numbers come from?

choices.label: Huntress sets and enforces them globally.
choices.rationale: Huntress doesn't enforce an SLA on your side.

choices: Your MSP's contract with each customer, with after-hours rules set by your MSP's policy.

choices.label: The customer sets them at onboarding.
choices.rationale: The customer agrees at contract; they aren't unilaterally set per ticket.

choices.label: The senior on shift decides them ad-hoc.
choices.rationale: Ad-hoc SLAs aren't a thing.

questions.explanation: SLAs are partner-specific. Read your MSP's document at least once.

questions.prompt: You read an incident: Low severity, but the Recommendation says "isolate the host immediately." What does the lesson suggest you do?

choices.label: Ignore the recommendation; if it's Low, you don't isolate.
choices.rationale: Ignoring the Recommendation is the cardinal mistake.

choices: Send a clarifying reply to the analyst — the tag and the text don't match, and it's worth confirming before acting.

choices.label: Isolate immediately because the Recommendation says so, ignore the mismatch.
choices.rationale: You should not just act; a 30-second reply costs nothing before something irreversible.

choices.label: Re-grade the severity to Critical to match the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: Re-grading is the SOC's call.

questions.explanation: Mismatch between tag and text is information. The right surface is a clarifying reply.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the lesson's example of a High-severity signal?
choices: A scheduled task running PowerShell with encoded commands.

choices.label: A ransomware canary trip.
choices.rationale: Critical example, not High.

choices.label: An autoruns entry from a legitimate-but-uncommon software vendor.
choices.rationale: Low example, not High.

choices.label: Mass deletion of files across a tenant.
choices.rationale: Critical example, not High.

questions.explanation: Encoded-PowerShell scheduled tasks sit in the High band alongside malicious inbox rules and unrecognised OAuth grants.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT a Critical-severity example from the lesson?

choices.label: Ransomware canary trip.
choices.rationale: Critical example.

choices.label: Mass deletion of files.
choices.rationale: Critical example.

choices.label: Lateral movement indicators.
choices.rationale: Critical example.

choices: A sign-in from a new country that the user later confirms.
questions.explanation: New-country sign-in with user confirmation is the lesson's Low example, not Critical.
